summary: Investing sounds simple. Start early, invest regularly, diversify, review, be patient. But if it’s so simple, why do so many people still fail to create wealth? In this episode of This or That with Bhartendra, I sit down with Mohit Bhatia, CEO of Bank of India Mutual Fund(ex–Franklin Templeton, DSP, Axis Bank, Canara Robeco), to break down why investing is simple but not easy – and what actually works for Indian investors.

Asset Allocation vs Diversification vs Timing (F1 Engine Analogy)
SIP Myths: Frequency vs Category – What Really Matters

seo.title: Why Investing Feels Hard (Even When It’s Simple)
seo.description: Investing sounds simple. Start early, invest regularly, diversify, review, be patient. But if it’s so simple, why do so many people still fail to create

takeaways.text: Investing is simple to describe but hard to live: start early, invest regularly, diversify, review, be patient, yet most people still struggle to build wealth.

Mohit Bhatia points to three real edges: an information edge (knowing what is happening), an analytical edge (understanding what it means), and a behavioural edge (controlling your emotions when markets are volatile).
Market returns are not the same as investor returns, so how you behave through the ups and downs often matters more than what the market itself does.
Asset allocation is your engine size in the F1 analogy, and tools like SIP, STP and SWP can turn volatility into a friend rather than a threat.
Sensible diversification beats spreading money across 10 to 20 random funds, and a good advisor or distributor earns their value mostly by controlling behaviour and simplifying choices.
